title: Rights
summary: A right is the trump card an individual holds against the world — a justified claim so strong that even the government and the majority must honor it, the idea that a person is owed certain things simply as a person. It is among the most powerful and the most contested moves in all of politics, splitting into deep arguments over where rights come from (nature, law, or social agreement) and which claims rise to that status.
hook: To say you have a right to speak is to say something startling: the public may not silence you even when silencing you would be useful and popular. A right is a kind of trump card. It defeats the ordinary math of politics, the counting of heads and weighing of benefits. The majority may want one thing, the government may find it convenient, and still the right stands in the way and says no. That is its strange power. It draws a line around a single person and tells the whole collective, however good its reasons, that it may not cross. Almost every cause in the modern world is now pressed as a right. Yet no one agrees on where rights come from, or on which claims deserve the name. The card is played everywhere, and its source is still unknown.
comparativeSummary: These vantage points are not arguing about whether rights are good. They are arguing about what a right fundamentally is. The natural-rights tradition treats a right as something discovered, inherent in the person, prior to and above any government that can only honor or violate it. The legal tradition answers that a right is something granted, the creation of an actual legal order, and that a right no law enforces is empty words. The third vantage treats rights as something built, the product of social agreement and a slowly widening moral consensus. Each names a different source as the real one, and each pays a price: the natural account cannot prove its foundation, the legal account cannot criticize an unjust law, and the consensus account cannot explain why a right binds those who never agreed.

beginner.theConcept:
Imagine a town that votes to silence one unpopular person. Almost everyone agrees. Yet we say they may not do it, because that person has a right to speak. The vote does not settle the matter. The right does.

That is the strange power at the heart of the idea. A right is a claim so strong that it beats the ordinary rules. Usually the side with more votes or more benefit wins. A right says no. It protects a single person even against the majority and the government.

Many rights also put a duty on other people. If you have a right to your property, others have a duty not to take it. If you have a right not to be tortured, the state has a duty never to torture you.

So a right is more than a wish or a good idea. It is a line drawn around a person that the collective may not cross, no matter how many people want it to.
beginner.problemItAddresses:
For most of history, a person was at the mercy of whoever ruled. A king could tax you, jail you, or kill you if it served his purpose. You were useful when wanted and disposable when not.

The idea of rights answers that danger. It says some things are owed to a person not as a gift or a kindness, but as their due. The powerful may not simply do as they please.

This idea has driven huge changes. It stood behind the limits placed on kings, who were told there were things even they could not do. It stood behind the end of slavery, the claim that no human being may be owned. It stands behind every demand that something be owed to a person not out of generosity, but because they had a right to it all along.

The power of the idea is that it protects the weak. A right does not depend on being liked or useful. It belongs to you simply because you are a person.
beginner.competingTraditions:
People who all believe in rights still argue fiercely, and one argument is about where rights come from.

One side says rights are part of being human. They exist before any government and stand above it. A ruler can recognize them or break them, but cannot create them or take them away. On this view, even a law allowing slavery would be wrong, because the right not to be owned was always there.

The other side says that talk of rights with no law behind it is empty. A right is only real when a legal system grants and enforces it. The thinker Jeremy Bentham mocked natural rights as nonsense, claims with nothing to back them. A right you cannot enforce is just a hope.

A third view splits the difference. Rights, it says, come from agreement and a sense of fairness that grows over time. They are built by societies, slowly, as people decide what each person is owed.
beginner.politicalStakes:
Here is what makes rights so explosive. Because a right beats the ordinary vote, calling something a right moves it out of normal politics. So the fiercest fights are over what counts as a right in the first place.

There is also a deep split over the kinds of rights. Some are freedoms from interference. The right to speak, to worship, to not be jailed without cause. These ask others mainly to leave you alone. Others are claims to be given something. The right to food, housing, health care, schooling. These ask others to provide, which costs money and effort, so they are far more contested.

Once a claim is named a right, the majority can no longer simply vote it down. That is why people fight so hard to attach the word to the things they care about, whether health care, property, or owning a gun.

This leads to a worry. If everything good becomes a right, the word may lose its force, and a society can end up with many rights that clash.
beginner.contestedHistoryC:
The bold language of natural rights took shape a few centuries ago. Writers began to argue that every person was born with certain rights, among them life, freedom, and the things they owned. These ideas were written into the great revolutionary declarations in America and France.

Yet the people who proclaimed these rights often denied them in practice. Many held slaves. They shut women out of public life. They gave rights to some and refused them to most of humanity. The grand claim and its daily betrayal lived side by side.

Critics attacked the idea from the start. Some called abstract rights a fiction, words with nothing solid behind them. Others, fighting for the excluded, turned the same language back on its authors, demanding the rights that had been kept for a few.

Some read this history as the slow spread of a true idea meant for everyone. Others see a particular tradition, born in one place, whose grand claims have served both to free people and to rule over them.
beginner.whereDebateStands:
Today the language of rights is everywhere. Almost every cause, on every side, is now stated as a rights claim. That alone would have amazed most of history. But the agreement is thinner than it looks.

The old questions are still open. Are rights discovered in human nature, or simply granted by law? People answer in opposite ways. Do claims to food, housing, and health care count as real rights, on the same footing as the right to speak? That is fiercely disputed.

Another live question is whether rights are the same for everyone, everywhere, or mostly a Western idea pressed onto other cultures. And when two rights collide, there is no agreed way to decide which wins.

There is even a worry that the idea has been too successful. If everything desirable becomes a right, the word may lose its force. People reach for it constantly, yet still cannot agree on what stands behind it.

general.theConcept:
When a court strikes down a law that a large majority voted for, because it violates a single person's rights, it shows what a right really is. A right is a claim that defeats ordinary politics. Most political questions are settled by counting: more votes, greater benefit, the will of the majority. A right interrupts that count. It says that some things may not be done to a person no matter how many people want them done, and no matter how useful the result would be.

The philosopher Ronald Dworkin captured this by calling rights trumps. In a card game, a trump beats a higher card of another suit. Even so, real legal systems still specify, limit, and balance rights when they collide. A right works the same way against the ordinary currency of politics. An individual's freedom to worship as they choose stands even when the public good, the convenience of officials, and the preference of the majority all point the other way. The right marks a boundary that numbers are not supposed to cross.

A right is also structured, not a vague good feeling but a claim that lays a determinate duty on others. The legal theorist Wesley Hohfeld showed that a right held by one person is invariably mirrored by a duty in someone else, so that your right to your land is matched by everyone else's obligation not to trespass upon it. What kinds of claims rise to this protected status, and why they should, is the deep argument the idea contains. To ask whether someone believes in rights is therefore to ask the wrong question; the genuine question is which claims qualify as rights, and where their authority ultimately comes from.
general.problemItAddresses:
Rights answer a permanent and ugly danger: that the powerful will sacrifice the individual to their purposes. Across most of recorded history, a person stood largely at the mercy of whoever ruled. Monarchs could tax, conscript, imprison, and kill, and the individual had no claim that could stop them. The person was an instrument, useful when wanted and discarded when not. This was not seen as an outrage. It was seen as the order of things.

The language of rights denies that order at the root. It marks out a protected zone for the individual into which the collective may not reach, however good its reasons. To have a right is to be owed something not as charity, which can be withdrawn, but as one's due, which cannot. This shifts the burden. The person no longer has to justify being left alone or being treated decently. Those who would interfere must justify the interference.

That single move has been the moral instrument behind enormous changes. It stands behind the limits placed on government, the insistence that there are things even a sovereign may not do. It stands behind the abolition of slavery, the claim that a human being cannot be owned whatever the law says. It stands behind every demand that some treatment is owed to a person as a matter of right rather than of mercy. Its strength is that it protects those who are weak, unpopular, or useless to the powerful, since a right does not depend on being any of those things. It belongs to the person as a person.
general.competingTraditions:
The traditions that divide thinking about rights are best read as rival answers to a single question: where does a right get its authority? The natural-rights tradition holds that rights are inherent in persons, prior to and above any government. On this view, associated with thinkers such as John Locke, a government does not create rights but finds them already there. It can recognize them or violate them, never invent them or abolish them. The strength of the view is moral force: it lets us say that a law permitting slavery or torture is unjust even if it is perfectly valid law, because it breaches a right that was always real. Its weakness is foundation. When pressed to show where these rights come from and how we know them, the natural-rights theorist must point to human nature, or reason, or God, and each answer is itself disputed.

The legal tradition answers that a right is whatever an actual legal order grants and enforces, and nothing more. Talk of rights with no law behind them is, in Jeremy Bentham's famous jibe, nonsense upon stilts. On this view a right is a creature of human institutions, real precisely because courts and officials will act on it. Its strength is hard-headedness: an enforceable right protects you, while a natural right that no one will uphold is a comfort and nothing more. Its weakness is the mirror image of the first view's strength. If a right is only what the law grants, then a regime that grants no rights violates none, and the slave under a slaveholding law has no right to complain of, only a wish.

A third tradition treats rights as the product of social agreement or an evolving moral consensus. Rights, on this account, are neither discovered in nature nor simply handed down by rulers. They are built by societies as people work out what each person is owed, and the list grows and changes over time. This explains the historical record well, since the rights a society recognizes plainly do shift. Its difficulty is binding force. If a right rests on agreement, it is unclear why it binds those who never agreed, or why a society that reaches a cruel consensus is wrong rather than merely different.

Cutting across the question of source is a question about kinds. Negative rights are freedoms from interference, the rights to speak, to worship, to not be tortured. They ask others mainly to refrain, to leave you alone, and so are often called cheaper to honor — though even they depend on courts and police to enforce. Positive rights are entitlements to goods, the rights to food, shelter, health care, and schooling. They ask others to provide, which requires resources and effort, and so are costlier and far more contested. And individual rights, held by each person alone, can collide with group rights claimed by peoples, minorities, or nations, whose claims to land, language, or self-rule do not always sit easily with the rights of the individuals inside them.
general.politicalStakes:
What makes rights so combustible is that declaring something a right moves it beyond ordinary politics. Because a right defeats the majority and the calculation of benefit, to win the label of right for a claim is to armor it against the legislature and the vote. To deny the label is to leave the claim to the ordinary push and pull of politics, where it can be traded away. This is why the fiercest battles are not over whether rights matter, but over what counts as one.

The pattern runs through almost every contested claim. Is access to health care a right, which a society is bound to secure, or a good policy goal, which it may pursue or not? Is property a near-absolute right against the state, or a privilege the community may heavily condition? Is there a right to own a gun, or a right to bodily autonomy, or a right to a clean environment? In each case, to establish the claim as a right is to take it off the bargaining table. That is precisely why the fights are so bitter, and why both sides reach for the word.

The split between kinds of rights sharpens the stakes. Negative rights, which ask only to be left alone, command wider agreement, since honoring them mostly requires restraint. Positive rights, which require others to provide goods at real cost, are harder won and more disputed, because one person's entitlement to a good is a claim on someone else's resources or labor. Behind all of it lies a worry that scholars across the spectrum take seriously, often called rights inflation. If every desirable thing is recast as a right, rights may lose the very force that made them special. A society can find itself with a crowd of competing rights, each claiming to trump the others, and no principled way to choose between them. The trump card loses its power when everyone holds a full hand of them.
general.contestedHistoryC:
The idea that a person holds claims against the world is ancient in its roots and modern in its sharpest form. Older traditions spoke of duties owed to persons and of limits on what rulers might justly do, and some held that a higher law stood above the king's command. But the crisp language of natural rights, of rights inhering in the individual and prior to government, crystallized in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. Thinkers spoke of the rights of man, of life, liberty, and property belonging to everyone by nature, and the claim was written into the great revolutionary charters of America and France.

The contradiction was glaring from the start. Many who proclaimed universal rights held enslaved people, excluded women from public life, and denied any rights to most of humanity. The principle was announced in the same breath in which it was withheld. Critics attacked the idea from opposite directions. Conservative thinkers dismissed abstract natural rights as dangerous fictions, claims with no basis in real law or settled custom. Radical critics charged that the grand language was a mask for the interests of property and the powerful. Yet the excluded soon turned the same language against its authors. If rights belonged to all by nature, then enslaved people, women, and colonized peoples could demand the rights that had been reserved for a few.

Scholars read this trajectory in two ways. One reading sees the slow realization of a true idea, the gradual extension of a principle that was always implicit in the original claim, with the excluders failing by their own professed standard. A competing reading resists this story. It argues that rights talk was born in a particular place and was often compatible with, even useful to, hierarchies of race and sex, so that the boundaries of the rights-bearer were actively drawn rather than merely overlooked. On this reading the same tradition has served both liberation and domination, and each extension was wrung from the powerful by struggle rather than conceded by the logic of the idea.
general.whereDebateStands:
The language of rights has become the common moral currency of the world. Nearly every cause, across the political spectrum and across borders, is now pressed as a rights claim, and that near-universal reach would have astonished most of human history. Yet the foundations of the idea remain as contested as ever, and the spread of the language has not settled the deep questions beneath it.

The oldest dispute is unresolved. Are rights discovered in human nature, standing above all law, or are they merely granted by legal systems that could in principle have granted otherwise? A second concerns kinds. Do positive rights to welfare, food, and health care stand on the same footing as negative rights to liberty, or are they goals of a different and weaker sort, since they depend on resources a society may lack? A third concerns reach. Are rights truly universal, the same for all humanity everywhere, or are they a particular tradition, often Western in origin, pressed onto cultures that order things differently? Defenders insist the universality is real and hard-won; critics answer that the claim of universality has itself been a vehicle of power.

Two further problems press hard. When rights collide, one person's against another's, or one group's against an individual's, there is no agreed procedure for deciding which prevails, and much of modern law is the unending work of adjudicating such conflicts. And there is the worry that the very success of rights talk has begun to drain the idea of its force. If everything that is wanted is recast as a right, the word may cease to mark anything special, and a society may be left with competing rights and no way to rank them. The idea has conquered the world's moral vocabulary while the question of what grounds it remains as open as it ever was.

advanced.theConcept: The distinctive logic of a right is best exposed not by defining it but by analyzing its structure, and the indispensable instrument here is Wesley Hohfeld's disaggregation of the word. Hohfeld showed that the single term right conceals at least four distinct legal positions: a claim, correlated with a duty in another; a liberty or privilege, the mere absence of a duty not to act; a power, the ability to alter legal relations; and an immunity, protection against another's power. The careless slide among these is the source of much confusion, since to assert a right may be to assert any of them. The morally weighty notion, the one that does the work in political argument, is the claim-right with its correlative duty, because it is the position that binds another party. Ronald Dworkin built on this in characterizing rights as trumps over background justifications of policy: a right, on his account, is precisely a consideration that cannot be defeated by the ordinary appeal to aggregate welfare, which is why a utilitarian calculation, however favorable, does not by itself settle a question once a right is in play. The deep claim is that a right marks a constraint on the pursuit of the collective good rather than a component of it. To treat a right as merely a very weighty interest, to be balanced against others on the same scale, is to dissolve the feature that distinguishes it.
advanced.problemItAddresses: The problem rights address is deeper than the abolition of arbitrary power, because the appeal to rights conceals a contest over what makes a claim authoritative, and the rival answers are not reconcilable by sharper definition. The will theory and the interest theory of rights divide on the most basic question of what a right protects. On the will theory, associated with H.L.A. Hart, a right secures a domain of choice, so that the right-holder is a small-scale sovereign empowered to waive or enforce a duty owed to them. This elegantly explains why rights can be exercised and surrendered, but it struggles to attribute rights to beings who cannot choose, infants, the severely incapacitated, and on some versions cannot ground inalienable rights at all, since an inalienable right is one the holder lacks the power to waive. On the interest theory, associated with Joseph Raz, a right exists where an aspect of a person's well-being is a sufficient reason to hold others under a duty. This extends rights naturally to those who cannot choose and accommodates inalienability, but at the cost of the tight correlation between right and duty, since not every important interest generates a determinate duty in a determinate agent. The dispute is not scholastic. It determines who can hold rights, whether rights can be waived, and how a right is individuated from the duties it grounds, and no neutral vantage adjudicates between protecting choice and protecting interest as the function of the institution.
advanced.competingTraditions: The confrontation between the natural and the positivist accounts is sharper than the textbook contrast of discovered versus granted suggests, because each position is strongest exactly where the other is weakest, and the disagreement is finally about what work the concept of a right is for. The positivist, following Bentham, holds that a right is the child of law and that imprescriptible natural rights are nonsense upon stilts, rhetorical claims dressed as legal ones. The force of this is not mere deflation. It is the observation that a right which no institution will vindicate offers the right-holder nothing, so that the practical reality of a right is its enforcement, and a theory that locates rights outside enforceable law has explained their dignity at the price of their efficacy. The natural-rights theorist answers with the case that positivism cannot accommodate: the morally monstrous but procedurally valid law. If rights are simply what the legal order grants, then a regime that legally licenses atrocity violates no rights, and the victim's complaint is reduced to a preference that the law were otherwise. This is the point at which the Nuremberg principle, that there are wrongs so grave that the cover of domestic legality is no defense, presupposes a standard above enacted law. The third tradition, locating rights in social agreement or evolving consensus, is sometimes offered as a synthesis, but it inherits a distinct difficulty. A consensus theory accounts elegantly for the manifest historical mutability of recognized rights, yet it cannot easily explain the normativity that rights claims assert, the sense in which a society's settled consensus can itself be condemned as a violation of rights. If the consensus is constitutive of the right, there is no foothold from which to criticize the consensus, which is the very thing reformers claim to do when they assert a right not yet recognized.
advanced.politicalStakes: The negative-positive distinction is invoked far more often than it is defended, and the analysis matters because the favored asymmetry between the two does not survive scrutiny. The standard view treats negative rights, freedoms from interference, as cheap, perfect, and universally assignable, since they require only that everyone refrain, while positive rights, entitlements to provision, are dismissed as costly, imperfect, and indeterminate in their addressees. Onora O'Neill and others have pressed the objection from the duty side: a right is empty unless the correlative duty is allocated to some identifiable agent, and negative rights face this problem too, since a right not to be assaulted does the holder no good without an institutional apparatus, police, courts, prisons, that is itself expensive and provided. The supposedly costless negative right turns out to presuppose a positive provision, which collapses the clean moral priority often claimed for it. The deeper stake concerns the relation between individual and group rights, where the conflict is not between expense and restraint but between two bearers of the protected status. A claim of collective self-determination, of a people to its language, territory, or political form, can license the subordination of individual members, the dissident, the woman, the apostate, whose individual rights the group asserts the authority to override in the name of its own. No appeal to rights in the abstract resolves this, because each side is a rights claim, and the question of which bearer is fundamental, the person or the people, is precisely what is in dispute. The honest description is that rights talk supplies the vocabulary of the conflict without supplying its resolution, and the recurrent danger, rights inflation, is structural rather than rhetorical: as more goods are cast as rights, the trumping force that made the concept valuable is diluted across an expanding field of mutually defeating claims.
advanced.contestedHistoryC: The intellectual history of rights exhibits a recurring structure in which a universalizing idiom and a restricted practice are asserted together, and the friction between them becomes the engine of subsequent argument. The seventeenth and eighteenth century crystallization of subjective natural rights, of rights as properties of the individual rather than as objective rightness of an order, was a genuine conceptual mutation whose dating and authorship remain contested, with some tracing a subjective rights vocabulary to medieval canon law and others to the early modern natural-law writers. What is clearer is that the revolutionary charters proclaimed the rights of man while their architects maintained slaveholding, patriarchal exclusion, and propertied franchises, and the question of how to read this is not merely retrospective bookkeeping. One reading, broadly progressivist, treats the subsequent extensions, abolition, enfranchisement, decolonization, as the working out of a logic the founding premise already contained, so that the excluders stood condemned by their own principle. A competing reading argues that the boundaries of the rights-bearing subject were actively constructed, that the universal idiom was frequently instrumental to the very hierarchies it appeared to indict, and that each extension was the contingent outcome of mobilization by the excluded rather than the unfolding of an idea. The critique sharpened in the conservative and the radical registers at once. Edmund Burke attacked abstract metaphysical rights in favor of concrete inherited liberties, while a later materialist critique dismissed the rights of man as the rights of the egoistic, property-holding individual of bourgeois society. That two such opposed traditions converged on the charge of abstraction is itself significant, and the choice among these readings shapes whether one expects residual exclusions to dissolve under the pressure of the principle or to persist until they are politically contested.
advanced.whereDebateStands: The contemporary frontier has moved past the foundational standoff that organized earlier debate, less by resolving it than by relocating the action to where rights are specified, conflicted, and bounded. A first front concerns the very intelligibility of rights without correlative duties. The proliferation of declared rights, many of them positive and many addressed to no determinate duty-bearer, has revived the objection that a right whose correlative obligation is unallocated is at best a manifesto right, an aspiration in the grammatical clothing of an entitlement, and the worry that such inflation erodes the concept's force has become a serious theoretical position rather than a mere complaint. A second front concerns the conflict of rights. Once it is conceded that genuine rights routinely collide, the picture of a right as an absolute side-constraint gives way to models of prima facie or specified rights whose content is fixed only after the conflict is adjudicated, which threatens to reintroduce the very balancing that the trumping conception was meant to exclude, and to relocate the substantive decision into the act of specification. A third front concerns scope and source together. The charge that the universal rights tradition is a particular, often Western, inheritance pressed upon other cultures has matured beyond simple relativism into a more searching question about whether a genuinely cross-cultural foundation is available, or whether the universality of rights is a contingent achievement to be defended politically rather than a fact to be demonstrated philosophically. The common thread is that the once-stable assumptions have come undone at both ends: neither the source of a right's authority nor the identity of its bearer nor the determinacy of its correlative duty can now be taken as given, and the trump card, played in every modern political contest, is wielded by a hand whose warrant remains genuinely unsettled.

foundation.theStory: Rights matter not because the idea is settled but because it is the most powerful and most contested move in modern politics. To call something a right is to claim that it cannot be overridden by majority preference or by the general benefit, that a single person holds it against the whole collective, including the state. Nearly every cause is now pressed in this language, a near-universal reach that conceals deep disagreement, because the agreement dissolves the moment one asks where rights come from and which claims deserve the name. Understanding the concept means seeing that a right is a justified claim laying a duty on others, that its distinctive power is to trump the ordinary calculation of the common good, and that the rival accounts of its source, nature, law, or social agreement, are genuinely incompatible rather than different emphases.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern the source of rights, the kinds of rights, and their reach. On source, the natural-rights tradition holds that rights are inherent in persons and prior to government, while the legal tradition holds that a right is only what an actual legal order grants and enforces, with Bentham dismissing natural rights as nonsense upon stilts, and a third view grounds rights in social agreement or evolving consensus. On kinds, negative rights are freedoms from interference that ask others only to refrain, while positive rights are entitlements to goods that ask others to provide and so are costlier and more disputed; individual rights can also collide with the rights claimed by groups. On reach, the question is whether rights are genuinely universal or a particular tradition pressed on different cultures. Running through all of it is the worry of rights inflation, that calling every desirable thing a right may drain the idea of its force.
foundation.whyItMatters: Rights connect almost every major political controversy: free speech, property, health care, gun ownership, bodily autonomy, the treatment of minorities, and the claims of peoples to self-rule are all, at their deepest level, disputes about whether a claim is a right and where its authority comes from. Grasping the concept's internal divisions is the defense against a common confusion, in which two sides accuse each other of betraying rights when in fact both affirm rights and mean different things by them. It also illuminates the link between rights and adjacent ideas, especially liberty, which the freedoms-from-interference protect, justice, the broader ideal that rights serve, and the procedures that make a right enforceable rather than merely declared.
questions: A right is said to trump the common good, so that the majority cannot override it merely because doing so would be useful or popular. What would be lost if rights were treated instead as very weighty interests to be balanced against other goods on the same scale?

The right to free speech asks others only to leave you alone, while the right to health care asks others to provide something at real cost. Are these two claims rights in the same sense, and if not, what is the difference between them?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The modern analysis of rights is organized by several distinct contributions. Wesley Hohfeld's Fundamental Legal Conceptions (1919) disaggregated the single word right into claim, liberty, power, and immunity, supplying the framework that shows a claim-right is correlated with a duty in another. Ronald Dworkin's Taking Rights Seriously (1977) advanced the influential thesis that rights are trumps over appeals to the general welfare, the feature that distinguishes a right from a mere interest. The contest over the function of rights runs between the will theory, defended by H.L.A. Hart, on which a right protects a domain of choice, and the interest theory, defended by Joseph Raz, on which a right protects an aspect of well-being sufficient to ground a duty. On the source of rights, the natural-rights tradition associated with John Locke confronts the legal positivism of Jeremy Bentham, whose 'Anarchical Fallacies' dismissed natural rights as nonsense upon stilts. Onora O'Neill has pressed the question of whether rights are coherent without specified duty-bearers, a challenge that bears directly on the status of positive rights.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The core disagreements here are conceptual and normative rather than empirical, so evidence constrains the debate without settling it. Empirical inquiry can establish what actually follows from a given rights regime, how courts in fact adjudicate conflicts between rights, whether declaring a positive right to a good improves its provision, and how the recognized list of rights has changed over time. Such findings can show that a conception of rights has implications its proponents may not endorse, and the historical record decisively refutes any claim that the recognized set of rights has been stable. What evidence cannot determine is which account of a right's source is correct, because that is a question about the grounds of authority rather than about fact. The case of the procedurally valid but morally monstrous law, for instance, is a test of whether rights stand above enacted law, and no measurement resolves whether the victim of such a law has had a right violated or merely a preference frustrated.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong argument about rights must first specify which conception of a right it is using, the source from which the right draws its authority and the function the right is taken to serve, since claims that leave this implicit tend to equivocate between a natural claim and a legal one, or between protecting choice and protecting interest. It must then confront the hardest case for its own view rather than defining it away. A defender of natural rights should explain how the foundational claim is known and why a being's lacking the relevant capacity does not simply remove the right; a positivist should explain what the victim of a valid but atrocious law is complaining of, if not a right. The most defensible positions tend to begin from a concrete wrong, a specific domination, exclusion, or atrocity, and reason toward the conception of rights that best accounts for why it is a wrong, rather than deducing entitlements from an abstract principle announced in advance. A position that treats every desirable good as self-evidently a right, or every unenforced claim as self-evidently empty, has not yet done the analytic work the concept demands.
questions: Bentham held that a right with no law behind it is nonsense upon stilts, while the natural-rights tradition holds that a valid law permitting atrocity violates rights that were always real. Each view is strongest where the other is weakest. What would a defender of one have to concede to the other, and can any third account hold both strengths at once?

A people's claim to self-determination is a group right, but honoring it can license the group to override the rights of individual members who dissent. When an individual right and a group right collide, is there any principled way to decide which is fundamental, or does the appeal to rights only restate the conflict?

research.topic: The structure of a right as a claim correlated with a duty
research.content: It is well established in legal and political philosophy that the single word 'right' denotes several distinct positions, and that the morally central one is the claim-right correlated with a duty in another party. Wesley Hohfeld's analysis distinguishing claims, liberties, powers, and immunities is the standard framework, and Ronald Dworkin's characterization of rights as trumps over appeals to aggregate welfare is the standard expression of what makes a right more than a weighty interest. The taxonomy of natural versus legal rights as accounts of source, and of negative versus positive and individual versus group rights as accounts of kind, is reflected across the major reference treatments of the concept. These distinctions are not themselves contested; what is contested is which account of source is correct and how the kinds are to be ranked.

research.topic: Rights inflation and the demand for specified duty-bearers
research.content: A substantial body of recent work presses the objection that a proliferating list of declared rights, many of them positive and addressed to no determinate duty-bearer, threatens to erode the concept's distinctive force. Onora O'Neill and others argue that a right is incoherent or merely aspirational unless the correlative obligation is allocated to an identifiable agent, which bears directly on the contested status of positive rights to goods such as food, housing, and health care. Whether such 'manifesto rights' are genuine rights, defective rights, or aspirations misdescribed in the grammar of entitlement remains actively disputed, as does the related claim that the inflation of rights dilutes the trumping force that made the concept valuable. The debate is open and unsettled.

research.topic: Whether rights have a foundation independent of law and agreement
research.content:
Whether there is a sound, non-circular justification for the conviction that persons hold rights prior to and above any legal order cannot be settled by the available philosophical arguments, and the question is genuinely open rather than merely difficult.

What evidence would resolve it: a demonstration either that some property of persons grounds rights independently of any institution recognizing them, or that the entire content of rights can be derived from positive law and social agreement without remainder, in a way that still accounts for the conviction that an atrocious valid law violates rights.

Why that is unavailable: the dispute concerns the grounds of normative authority rather than any fact, and the candidate intuitions pull in opposite directions and cannot be adjudicated by observation. The intuition that a procedurally valid law licensing atrocity nonetheless violates a right supports a foundation above enacted law; the intuition that an unenforceable right offers its holder nothing supports a wholly institutional account. No measurement decides between them, because each describes how rights ought to be grounded, not how the world is.

What can be responsibly inferred: that the commitment to rights functions as a near-foundational premise in modern moral and political thought rather than a conclusion derived from deeper agreed premises; that defensible theories exist on the natural, the legal, and the consensus side; and that the choice among them has real downstream consequences for who can hold rights and whether an unjust law can be condemned as a violation.

What must remain genuinely unknowable: whether rights exist in a sense independent of any order or agreement that affirms them, or whether, as some argue, the conviction rests finally on a commitment, secular or religious, that argument can elaborate and stabilize but cannot fully vindicate from outside.

comparativeNarrative.name: Rights as Discovered
comparativeNarrative.content: This tradition treats a right as inherent in the person, prior to any government and above it. A ruler does not create rights but finds them already there, and can only honor them or break them. The great achievement was the claim that there are things no sovereign may do, that the individual carries a standing the state must respect. What this vantage sees is moral force. It lets us say that a law permitting slavery or torture is unjust even when it is perfectly valid law, because it breaches a right that was always real. What it is pressed hardest on is foundation. Asked where these rights come from, it points to human nature, reason, or God, each itself disputed, so the strongest claim rests on the weakest ground.

comparativeNarrative.name: Rights as Granted
comparativeNarrative.content: This vantage holds that a right is whatever an actual legal order grants and enforces, and nothing more. A right that no institution will uphold is, in the famous jibe, nonsense upon stilts, a claim with nothing to back it. The strength here is hard-headed. An enforceable right protects you, while a natural right no one will act on is a comfort and not a shield. This vantage sees how much of a right's reality lies in its enforcement, in courts and officials. What it is pressed hardest on is the monstrous but valid law. If a right is only what the law grants, then a regime that legally licenses atrocity violates no rights, and the victim is left with a wish, not a right that was broken.

comparativeNarrative.name: Rights as Built
comparativeNarrative.content: A third vantage treats rights as neither found in nature nor handed down by rulers, but built by societies as people work out what each person is owed. The list of recognized rights plainly grows and shifts over time, and this vantage explains that record better than the others, since it expects rights to change as moral consensus changes. What it sees clearly is that rights are human achievements, won and revised through struggle rather than discovered whole. What it is pressed hardest on is binding force. If a right rests on agreement, it is unclear why it binds those who never agreed, and harder still to say why a cruel consensus is wrong rather than merely different, since the consensus meant to ground the right cannot also judge it.

rabbitHole.label: Human Rights
rabbitHole.reason: Human rights is the modern universalization of the idea of a right into a global code, the postwar attempt to fix certain rights as owed to every person and enforceable across borders. It is the most consequential institutional form the concept of a right has taken, and the place where the universality debate is fought most directly.

rabbitHole.label: Liberty
rabbitHole.reason: Liberty is the value that negative rights are built to protect: the freedoms from interference, to speak, to worship, to not be coerced, are the paradigm rights against the state. Understanding what freedom is and which freedoms matter is the prerequisite for seeing what those rights guard.

rabbitHole.label: Justice
rabbitHole.reason: Justice is the broader ideal that rights serve and that frames what each person is owed. Whether a claim rises to the status of a right, and how rights are ranked when they collide, are questions answered only from within a larger theory of what justice requires.

rabbitHole.label: Natural Law
rabbitHole.reason: The natural-law tradition is the deepest root of the claim that rights are inherent in persons and stand above any government's command. It supplies the framework on which the natural-rights account of a right's source is built, and the standard against which an unjust law is judged.

rabbitHole.label: Due Process
rabbitHole.reason: Due process is the cluster of procedural rights, to a fair hearing, to notice, to an impartial tribunal, that make other rights enforceable rather than merely declared. A right with no procedure to vindicate it is close to the empty claim the legal tradition warns against.

rabbitHole.label: Property
rabbitHole.reason: Property is among the oldest and most contested of rights, named in the founding declarations alongside life and liberty, and the clearest case of a single right that some treat as near-absolute against the state and others as a privilege the community may heavily condition.

rabbitHole.label: Equality
rabbitHole.reason: Equality is the sibling value that rights both serve and strain against: the idea of universal human rights is downstream of basic moral equality, yet individual rights can collide with demands for equal outcomes, so the two concepts are best understood against each other.

rabbitHole.label: The Rule of Law
rabbitHole.reason: The rule of law is the condition under which rights become more than paper: a regime of known, general, enforceable rules that binds the powerful and gives a right its purchase. The legal tradition's insistence that a right is what an order will actually uphold depends on it.

reference.author: Wesley Newcomb Hohfeld
reference.title: Fundamental Legal Conceptions as Applied in Judicial Reasoning
reference.annotation: The source of the disaggregation the entry builds on throughout: that the single word 'right' conceals claim, liberty, power, and immunity, and that the morally central position is the claim-right correlated with a duty in another. The indispensable analytical instrument for showing what a right structurally is.

reference.author: Ronald Dworkin
reference.title: Taking Rights Seriously
reference.annotation: The developed statement of rights as trumps over appeals to the general welfare that the entry uses for its central claim: that a right is precisely a consideration the ordinary calculation of the common good is not permitted to defeat, which is what distinguishes a right from a mere weighty interest.

reference.author: Jeremy Bentham
reference.title: Anarchical Fallacies
reference.annotation: The source of the 'nonsense upon stilts' jibe and of the legal-positivist case the entry sets against natural rights: that a right is the child of law, real only where an institution will enforce it, so that an imprescriptible natural right is a rhetorical claim dressed as a legal one.

reference.author: John Locke
reference.title: Two Treatises of Government
reference.annotation: The classic statement of natural rights to life, liberty, and property as pre-political claims that government is instituted to protect, which the entry uses to anchor the tradition holding that rights are discovered and inherent rather than granted by any legal order.

reference.author: H.L.A. Hart
reference.title: Are There Any Natural Rights?
reference.annotation: The leading statement of the will theory the advanced layer relies on, that a right secures a protected domain of choice in which the holder may waive or enforce a duty, together with the difficulties this raises for the rights of those who cannot choose and for inalienable rights.

reference.author: Joseph Raz
reference.title: The Morality of Freedom
reference.annotation: The developed statement of the interest theory the entry sets against the will theory: that a right exists where an aspect of a person's well-being is a sufficient reason to hold others under a duty, which extends rights to those who cannot choose at the cost of the tight right-duty correlation.

reference.author: Onora O'Neill
reference.title: The Dark Side of Human Rights
reference.annotation: The source of the duty-side objection the entry uses to question the negative-positive asymmetry and to frame rights inflation: that a right is empty unless its correlative obligation is allocated to an identifiable agent, a problem that afflicts unallocated positive rights most sharply.

reference.author: Jeremy Waldron (ed.)
reference.title: Theories of Rights
reference.annotation: The standard collection mapping the major theoretical disputes the entry draws on, including the will-versus-interest debate, the trumping conception, and the conflict between accounts that locate rights in nature, in law, and in social agreement.

reference.author: Brian Tierney
reference.title: The Idea of Natural Rights
reference.annotation: The historical study the contested-history layer leans on for the disputed origins of subjective natural rights, including the case for a medieval canon-law vocabulary of individual rights well before the early modern natural-law writers, which complicates any simple seventeenth-century dating.

commerce.title: Rights: A Very Short Introduction
commerce.author: David A. J. Richards (or the relevant author in the VSI series)
commerce.note: The gentlest way in for a reader who has never opened a book on the subject. The short introductions in this series lay out what a right is, where the natural and legal accounts part ways, and why negative and positive rights are treated so differently, all in plain language.

commerce.title: Taking Rights Seriously
commerce.author: Ronald Dworkin
commerce.note: Read this once the basic positions make sense. Dworkin's argument that rights are trumps over the general welfare is the single most useful idea for grasping why a right is more than a strong preference, and the book is demanding but written with real clarity.

commerce.title: The Last Utopia: Human Rights in History
commerce.author: Samuel Moyn
commerce.note: For the reader who wants to see how the abstract idea of a right became the global language of human rights, and to question the comfortable story of its long, steady rise. Pair it with a defender of the older natural-rights lineage to stay even-handed.

commerce.title: Theories of Rights
commerce.author: Jeremy Waldron (editor)
commerce.note: The academic anchor for anyone going deep. This collection gathers the key essays behind every dispute the subject contains, the will-versus-interest debate, rights as trumps, and the clash between natural and legal accounts of where a right comes from.

commerce.title: The Idea of Natural Rights
commerce.author: Brian Tierney
commerce.note: Read this for the deep history of where the very notion of an individual right came from. Tierney makes the case that a vocabulary of subjective rights existed in medieval law long before the famous revolutionary declarations, which unsettles the standard timeline.

popularCulture.title: 'You have rights'
popularCulture.description: The phrase is a reflex in films, shows, and daily speech. A character in trouble insists that they have rights, and everyone knows it means a line the powerful cannot cross. It carries the real idea well, that a person is owed something as their due. It also flattens it, since people reach for the word to dress up almost any want, blurring a true right and a strong wish.

popularCulture.title: 'Nonsense upon stilts'
popularCulture.description: Jeremy Bentham's mocking line about natural rights still gets quoted to deflate grand talk of inborn rights. It is sharp and memorable, and it names a real challenge: a right no one will enforce may protect no one. But the phrase is often flung as a put-down without its argument, used to wave away rights talk in general rather than to make Bentham's careful point that a right needs a law behind it.

popularCulture.title: 'Inalienable rights'
popularCulture.description: The idea that some rights can never be taken or given away entered everyday speech through revolutionary declarations and stuck. It thrills because it makes certain protections feel beyond reach of any power. It also hides a hard question the phrase never answers: where these unbreakable rights come from, and why a right you cannot even surrender yourself should bind a government that never granted it.

popularCulture.title: Reading someone their rights
popularCulture.description: Countless crime dramas show an arrest with the warning that you have the right to remain silent. The scene made the idea of a right vivid and familiar to millions, a thing an officer must honor on the spot. It also narrows the picture, since it suggests rights are mainly fine print recited by police, rather than the much larger claim that a person holds protections against the whole power of the state.

popularCulture.title: Everything as a right
popularCulture.description: Almost every cause is now stated as a right. A right to privacy, a right to a job, a right to be forgotten online, a right to own a gun. The habit shows how powerful the word has become, since claiming a right ends an argument fast. The trouble is that when everything wanted becomes a right, the word stops marking anything special, and rights collide with no clear way to choose.
